title: Antiphospholipid Syndrome
authors: Haralampos M. Moutsopoulos, Maria G. Tektonidou

items.title: Understanding the Major Autoantibodies in Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS)
items.content: APS is characterized by autoantibodies against phospholipid-binding proteins, primarily $ \beta_2 $-glycoprotein I and prothrombin. These antibodies contribute to thrombosis risk, with anti-domain I IgG being particularly associated with increased thrombotic events.
keyPoints: APS involves autoantibodies against phospholipid-binding proteins like $ \beta_2 $-glycoprotein I (43-kDa plasma apolipoprotein) and prothrombin.
Anti-domain I IgG antibodies are linked to increased thrombotic risk.
The antithrombotic function of $ \beta_2 $GPI depends on its fifth domain.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · Antiphospholipid Syndrome | DEFINITIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Epidemiology and Prevalence of Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS)
items.content: APS has an incidence of 1–2 cases per 100,000 persons annually, with a prevalence of 40–50 per 100,000 in the general population. APL antibodies are found in 1–5% of the general population and increase with age.
keyPoints: Incidence of APS: 1–2 cases per 100,000 persons per year.
Prevalence of APS: 40–50 per 100,000 in the general population.
APL antibodies prevalence: 1–5% in the general population, increases with age.
One-third of SLE patients have APL antibodies; 10–15% develop APS.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · Antiphospholipid Syndrome | DEFINITIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Mechanism of Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS) Pathogenesis
items.content: The pathogenesis of APS involves endothelial injury induced by infections, oxidative stress, and physical stresses such as surgery or trauma. This occurs in individuals with specific genetic backgrounds, particularly those associated with alleles within the HLA locus. The injury leads to increased apoptosis of vessel endothelial cells, autoantigen presentation, and initiation of an autoimmune response. Binding of antiphospholipid antibodies (aPL) to disrupted endothelial cells results in a proinflammatory and prothrombotic state characterized by activation of monocytes and platelets, production of adhesion molecules and cytokines, complement and neutrophil activation, NET formation, and thrombus development. Recent studies suggest that type I interferon pathway activation and an imbalance between type I and III interferons may contribute to thrombotic events and obstetric complications in APS.
keyPoints: Endothelial injury induced by infections, oxidative stress, surgery, or trauma
Genetic predisposition associated with HLA locus alleles
Increased apoptosis of endothelial cells leading to autoantigen presentation
Proinflammatory and prothrombotic state involving monocytes, platelets, adhesion molecules, cytokines, complement, neutrophils, NET formation, and thrombus development
Type I interferon pathway activation and imbalance between type I and III interferons as potential mechanisms
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.title: Clinical Manifestations of Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS)
items.content: APS presents with a spectrum of clinical manifestations including venous or arterial thrombosis, microvascular events, and pregnancy morbidity. Venous thrombosis often occurs in the lower extremities leading to pulmonary emboli, while arterial thrombosis commonly affects cerebral arteries causing transient ischemic attacks, strokes, migraines, or cognitive dysfunction. Microvascular manifestations include livedo reticularis, aPL-nephropathy with hypertension and proteinuria, and rare conditions like myocardial disease and adrenal hemorrhage. Pregnancy morbidity in APS may result in prefetal or fetal death, preeclampsia, eclampsia, and placental insufficiency. Cardiac valve manifestations include Libman-Sacks endocarditis characterized by small valve vegetations. Hematologic features are thrombocytopenia and autoimmune hemolytic anemia, while musculoskeletal symptoms can involve arthralgias/arthritis and avascular bone necrosis.
keyPoints: Venous or arterial thrombosis, microvascular events, pregnancy morbidity
Deep venous thrombosis leading to pulmonary emboli; cerebral venous thrombosis causing intracranial hypertension and focal deficits
Arterial thrombosis affecting brain arteries with TIA, stroke, migraines, cognitive dysfunction
Microvascular manifestations: livedo reticularis, aPL-nephropathy (hypertension, proteinuria), myocardial disease, adrenal hemorrhage
Pregnancy morbidity: prefetal/fetal death, preeclampsia, eclampsia, placental insufficiency
Cardiac valve manifestations: Libman-Sacks endocarditis with small vegetations
Hematologic features: thrombocytopenia, autoimmune hemolytic anemia
Musculoskeletal symptoms: arthralgias/arthritis, avascular bone necrosis
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.title: Criteria for APS Diagnosis: ACR/EULAR Classification
items.content: The diagnosis of Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS) requires meeting specific criteria set by the 2023 ACR/EULAR classification. This involves accumulating at least three points from both clinical and laboratory domains, with exclusions for conditions that have other more likely explanations than APS.
keyPoints: Presence of thrombosis, cerebral vascular accidents in individuals <55 years, or pregnancy morbidity suggests considering APS diagnosis.
ACR/EULAR criteria require 3 points each from clinical and laboratory domains.
Clinical domains: venous thromboembolism, arterial thrombosis, microvascular events, obstetric complications, cardiac valve disease, hematologic manifestations.
Laboratory domains: LA, aCL, anti-β2GPI antibodies at moderate (40-79 units) or high titers (≥80 units) on two occasions 12 weeks apart.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of APS: Exclusion of Other Conditions
items.content: When diagnosing APS, it is crucial to differentiate from other conditions that could cause similar symptoms. This involves ruling out inherited or acquired thrombophilia, Coombs-positive hemolytic anemia, and thrombocytopenia.
keyPoints: Differential diagnosis includes exclusion of other causes of thrombophilia, Coombs-positive hemolytic anemia, and thrombocytopenia.
Livedo reticularis may indicate disorders affecting the vascular wall (e.g., atherosclerosis, polyarteritis nodosa) or lumen.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which autoantibodies are primarily associated with antiphospholipid syndrome (APS)?
options: Lupus anticoagulant and anti-cardiolipin IgM antibodies
items.explanation: $\beta_2$-glycoprotein I and prothrombin are the major autoantibodies detected in APS patients.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · Antiphospholipid Syndrome | DEFINITIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome (CAPS) characterized by?
options: Recurrent thrombosis in a single organ
Thromboembolic disease involving three or more organs rapidly
Presence of anti-domain I IgG antibodies only
Prolonged clotting time corrected by normal plasma
items.explanation: CAPS is characterized by thromboembolic disease affecting simultaneously three or more organs.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · Antiphospholipid Syndrome | DEFINITIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which antibodies are measured using standardized ELISA in APS?
items.explanation: Anticardiolipin and anti-$\beta_2$GPI IgG/IgM antibodies are measured by standardized ELISA.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · Antiphospholipid Syndrome | DEFINITIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND LABORATORY FINDINGS
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a proposed mechanism for thrombotic events in antiphospholipid syndrome (APS)?
options: Activation of the type I interferon pathway
Imbalance between type I and III interferons
Increased apoptosis of vessel endothelial cells
items.explanation: Direct inhibition of platelet aggregation is not mentioned as a mechanism for thrombotic events in APS.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND LABORATORY FINDINGS
items.question: Which clinical manifestation of APS involves the formation of painful papules or lower limb ulcers?
items.explanation: Livedoid vasculopathy presents with painful papules or lower limb ulcers.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND LABORATORY FINDINGS
items.question: What is a common site for deep venous thrombosis in APS?
items.explanation: Deep venous thrombosis occurs primarily in the lower extremities.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.question: According to the 2023 ACR/EULAR classification criteria for APS, how many points from clinical domains are required to classify a patient with antiphospholipid syndrome?
items.explanation: The classification requires at least 3 points from clinical domains.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following laboratory tests is NOT part of the APS classification criteria?
items.explanation: Rheumatoid factor is not part of the APS classification criteria.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: For a diagnosis of APS, how many times must antibodies be measured at moderate or high titers?
items.explanation: Antibodies must be measured on two occasions 12 weeks apart.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: The incidence of APS is estimated to be 40–50 cases per 100,000 persons per year.
items.explanation: The incidence of APS is estimated at 1–2 cases per 100,000 persons per year.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · Antiphospholipid Syndrome | DEFINITIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: One-third of patients with SLE possess antiphospholipid antibodies.
items.explanation: Approximately one-third of patients with SLE have these antibodies.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · Antiphospholipid Syndrome | DEFINITIONS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND LABORATORY FINDINGS
items.statement: Pulmonary hypertension can result from thrombosis of the pulmonary arteries in APS.
items.explanation: Thrombosis of the pulmonary arteries leads to pulmonary hypertension.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND LABORATORY FINDINGS
items.statement: APS is associated with microvascular manifestations such as myocardial disease confirmed by magnetic resonance imaging.
items.explanation: Myocardial disease can be a rare microvascular manifestation of APS.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · ■ PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.statement: Livedo reticularis is considered in the diagnosis of APS even if other explanations are more likely.
items.explanation: Clinical manifestations with other equally or more likely explanations than APS will not be counted.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Differential diagnosis of APS includes exclusion of inherited causes of thrombophilia.
items.explanation: APS differential diagnosis involves excluding other inherited or acquired causes of thrombophilia.
items.reference: Antiphospholipid Syndrome · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: The presence of at least 3 points from laboratory domains is required to classify APS.
items.reason: According to the ACR/EULAR criteria, both clinical and laboratory domains require at least 3 points for classification.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
